title: Why Leadership Is a Behavior (Not a Title) + Credit Confidence for Women
guestBio: Elizabeth Taylor, known as @thecorporatebestie, is a leadership trainer, sales coach, and organizational development expert who specializes in helping high-performing women transition into management roles without burning out. She combines her expertise in leadership development with her background in the credit industry to help women build both professional confidence and financial literacy.
excerpt: Leadership isn't about your title—it's about how you show up. Elizabeth Taylor breaks down why women managers are the most burned-out group and shares the financial literacy skills every leader needs to build generational wealth.
metaDescription: Learn why leadership is a behavior, not a title. Elizabeth Taylor shares how women can lead without burnout plus credit confidence tips for financial freedom.
keyTakeaways: Leadership is fundamentally a behavior and mindset, not a position or title you hold.
Women managers between 35-45 experience the highest rates of burnout and need specific strategies to combat this epidemic.
Developing a clear leadership brand helps you show up consistently and confidently in your role.
Credit confidence is a crucial but underrated leadership skill that directly impacts your freedom and opportunities.
Being risk-aware rather than risk-averse allows women to make informed financial decisions with confidence.
The current generational wealth transfer presents unprecedented opportunities for women who are financially prepared.
Balancing ambition with rest and joy is essential for sustainable leadership success.

pullQuotes.text: Leadership isn't a title — it's a behavior.

timestamps.label: Why women managers are burning out

content:
What if everything you've been told about leadership is wrong? What if it's not about climbing the corporate ladder, collecting impressive titles, or managing the biggest team? Elizabeth Taylor, better known as @thecorporatebestie, is here to flip the script entirely.

In this eye-opening conversation with Aimee, Elizabeth reveals a truth that will reshape how you think about your career: leadership isn't a title you earn—it's a behavior you choose. And that choice? It can transform not just your professional life, but your financial future too.

The Burnout Epidemic Nobody's Talking About

Let's start with a sobering reality check. Women managers between the ages of 35 and 45 are experiencing burnout at unprecedented rates. You might be nodding your head right now, feeling seen in a way that's both validating and exhausting. Elizabeth doesn't just acknowledge this crisis—she provides a roadmap out of it.

The problem isn't that women are weak or incapable of handling leadership pressure. The issue is systemic. We're often promoted into management roles without proper training, expected to figure it out as we go, all while managing households, caring for aging parents, and trying to maintain some semblance of personal identity. Sound familiar?

Elizabeth's approach is refreshingly different. She believes that when you understand leadership as a behavior rather than a position, you can start showing up differently immediately. You don't need to wait for someone to hand you a fancy title or corner office. You can begin leading today, in whatever role you're in, with whatever resources you have.

Your Leadership Brand Is Your Superpower

Here's where Elizabeth's wisdom gets really practical. She talks about developing what she calls a "leadership brand"—the consistent way you show up, make decisions, and interact with others. This isn't about personal branding in the Instagram-influencer sense. This is about intentional consistency that builds trust and respect over time.

Think about the leaders you most admire. Chances are, you can predict how they'll handle certain situations because they've established clear patterns of behavior. They're not reinventing themselves every day or trying to be someone different in every meeting. They know who they are as leaders, and they show up authentically within that framework.

Your leadership brand becomes your anchor when everything else feels chaotic. When you're clear on your values, decision-making style, and communication approach, you can navigate challenges with confidence instead of second-guessing every choice.

The Money Conversation We're Not Having

But Elizabeth doesn't stop at traditional leadership advice. She brings something unique to the table: a deep understanding of how financial literacy intersects with leadership effectiveness. This is where the conversation gets really interesting—and really important.

She argues that credit confidence is one of the most underrated leadership skills, and she's absolutely right. When you understand credit, you understand risk assessment, financial planning, and strategic thinking. These are the exact skills that make you a better leader in any context.

More importantly, financial literacy gives you freedom. Freedom to take calculated risks in your career. Freedom to negotiate from a position of strength. Freedom to build the kind of generational wealth that changes everything for your family.

Risk-Aware, Not Risk-Averse

Elizabeth makes a crucial distinction that every woman needs to hear: there's a difference between being risk-averse and being risk-aware. Society has conditioned many women to avoid risk altogether, but that's not serving us. Smart leaders aren't afraid of risk—they're informed about it.

This mindset shift is profound. When you're risk-aware, you ask better questions. You gather more information. You make decisions based on data rather than fear. You start to see opportunities where others see obstacles.

This applies whether you're considering a career move, starting a business, making an investment, or even having difficult conversations at work. Risk-awareness gives you permission to be bold while still being smart.

The Wealth Transfer Moment

Here's something that might surprise you: we're in the middle of the largest generational wealth transfer in history. Elizabeth points out that women who are financially prepared for this moment have unprecedented opportunities ahead of them.

But preparation is key. You can't benefit from opportunities you don't understand or aren't positioned to take advantage of. This is why Elizabeth connects leadership development with financial education—they're not separate skills, they're complementary ones.

Permission to Rest and Lead

Perhaps the most radical thing Elizabeth advocates for is this: you can be ambitious and prioritize rest and joy. These aren't mutually exclusive. In fact, sustainable leadership requires both.

Too many women are burning out because they think leadership means constant sacrifice and perpetual hustle. Elizabeth offers a different model—one where taking care of yourself isn't selfish, it's strategic. When you're operating from a place of rest and joy, you make better decisions, communicate more effectively, and inspire others naturally.

This isn't about work-life balance in the traditional sense. It's about integration. It's about showing up as a whole person who happens to be a leader, rather than trying to compartmentalize yourself into unsustainable fragments.

The truth Elizabeth shares throughout this conversation is both simple and revolutionary: you already have what it takes to lead. You don't need permission from anyone else, another degree, or a different personality. You need clarity on your values, confidence in your abilities, and the courage to show up consistently as the leader you already are.

Leadership is a behavior. Financial literacy is power. And you? You're more capable than you know.

quiz.title: What's Your Leadership Style DNA?
quiz.description: Leadership isn't about your title—it's about how you show up every single day. Discover your natural leadership approach and how to leverage it for success.

questions.question: When you're facing a major decision at work, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Consider how this will impact each person on your team individually
Look for the strategic opportunity that others might be missing
Gather data and think through potential long-term consequences
Trust your gut while staying flexible about the outcome

questions.question: Your relationship with financial risk could best be described as:

options.text: I'll take risks for others' benefit, but rarely for my own financial gain
I see financial opportunities where others see obstacles
I prefer building wealth slowly through informed, steady choices
I adjust my financial strategy based on what life throws at me

questions.question: When someone asks you to describe your leadership style, you'd probably say:

options.text: I lead by caring deeply and making sure everyone feels supported
I lead by helping people see what's possible and inspiring action
I lead by being consistent, reliable, and following through on commitments
I lead by meeting people where they are and adjusting to what they need

questions.question: You're most likely to feel burned out when:

options.text: You've been giving so much to everyone else that you've forgotten about yourself
You're stuck in routines that don't challenge you or move the needle forward
You're being pressured to make quick decisions without proper analysis
You're forced into rigid structures that don't allow for flexibility

questions.question: Your ideal work environment would allow you to:

options.text: Build deep relationships and create a culture where everyone thrives
Think big, take calculated risks, and drive meaningful change
Work systematically toward clear goals with adequate resources
Pivot when needed and apply your skills to diverse challenges

questions.question: When it comes to balancing ambition with rest and joy, you:

options.text: Struggle to rest because you're always thinking about how to help others
Find joy in pursuing ambitious goals, but sometimes forget to celebrate wins
Believe rest and planning go hand-in-hand for sustainable success
Adapt your definition of balance based on what each season of life requires

results.title: The Fierce Protector
results.description: You lead with your heart and fierce loyalty to your team. Your superpower is creating psychological safety where others can thrive, but you often sacrifice your own needs in the process. You're the manager everyone wants to work for, yet you're probably experiencing that burnout epidemic Elizabeth talks about. This episode will show you how to channel your protective instincts into sustainable leadership that doesn't drain your energy.

results.title: The Strategic Visionary
results.description: You see possibilities everywhere and aren't afraid to take calculated risks to get there. Your natural leadership style is about painting the big picture and inspiring others to join your mission. You understand that leadership is a behavior, not a title, and you've probably been leading long before anyone gave you permission. This episode will help you develop the credit confidence and financial literacy to turn your bold visions into reality.

results.title: The Steady Builder
results.description: You believe in consistent progress over flashy moves, and your leadership style reflects that wisdom. You're naturally risk-aware rather than risk-averse, making thoughtful decisions that build long-term success. Your team trusts you because you show up the same way every day, creating that leadership brand Elizabeth emphasizes. This episode will validate your approach while showing you how to amplify your quiet confidence in bigger ways.

results.title: The Adaptive Navigator
results.description: You're the leader who thrives in uncertainty and helps others find their footing when everything feels chaotic. Your strength lies in reading situations quickly and adjusting your approach to meet the moment. You intuitively understand that leadership is about behavior and mindset, not hierarchy. This episode will give you frameworks to harness your adaptability while building the financial confidence to navigate any storm.
